question: The docs classify the Kafka connector as bidirectional. Which two components make it bidirectional?

options.text: A Kafka listener that forwards records to Zeebe, and a job worker that creates Kafka records every time a service task is executed.
A gRPC reverse proxy in front of Zeebe and a JDBC adapter to a Kafka offset table.
A REST endpoint that starts instances and a second REST endpoint that completes jobs.
A consumer that polls Zeebe for events and a producer that polls Kafka for events.

a.text: The docs describe the Kafka connector as bidirectional precisely because it pairs a Kafka listener that forwards Kafka records to Zeebe with a job worker that creates Kafka records whenever a service task is executed.

b.text: There is no gRPC reverse proxy or JDBC adapter involved; the connector uses Zeebe's client and a Kafka client.

c.text: The connector does not expose REST endpoints for starting or completing instances — its two halves are a Kafka listener and a Zeebe job worker.

d.text: The connector reacts to events (Kafka records arrive, Zeebe activates jobs), it does not poll Zeebe and Kafka in a tight loop.

explanation: An out-of-the-box connector can be uni- or bidirectional. The HTTP connector is one-way (job worker calls HTTP). The Kafka connector is bidirectional because it combines a Kafka listener that turns incoming records into Zeebe interactions with a Zeebe job worker that emits Kafka records from service tasks.

docs.title: Connecting the workflow engine with your world
